channel.title: Opensource Sunday – Running the Year
channel.description: Recent content in Running the Year on Opensource Sunday

item.title: Sample Calendar
item.description:
A full-year Confirmation schedule.

This is the master calendar we use. It covers every class session, the fall retreat, Confirmation Dessert, Confirmation Sunday, and the parent touchpoints in between. Copy it, shift the dates, and adapt to your own program.

Typical shape of a year

  • September — invites go out to rising 9th graders; Kickoff (Session 1A)
  • Early October — first parent/student class (Session 1B Covenant)
  • October–November — Sessions 2 (Trust) and 3 (Sin)
  • Mid-November — Fall Retreat (Friday–Sunday)
  • December — Sessions 4 (Lord) and 5 (Salvation), with a break for Christmas
  • January–February — Sessions 6 (Disciple), 7 (Love)
  • March — Session 8 (Teaching), Serve Saturday, Session 9 (Fellowship)
  • Early April — Holy Week reenactment; break for Easter
  • April–May — Sessions 10 (Breaking Bread), 11 (Prayer); statement-of-faith drafting
  • Late May — Session 12 (Crossing Over); Confirmation Dessert
  • End of May / early June — Confirmation Sunday (Children & Youth Sunday)

Downloads

📄
Downloadable templates. The source calendar spreadsheet and parent-facing PDF live in the source repo under the year they were used. Ask the maintainer to publish a recent year’s copy if you need one.

Planning tips

  • Sync with your church’s master calendar before you commit to dates. Mission trips, Holy Week events, and vacation-week breaks will eat your Sunday mornings.
  • Build in flex weeks. Snow days, graduations, and unexpected illnesses will cost you a session or two. Having 2–3 “no class” weeks scattered through the year gives you room.
  • Pair each class with its parent email. Send the email the week after, while the topic is fresh.
  • Put the fall retreat before Session 4. The retreat bonds the class; after it, the deep-cut sessions (Lord, Salvation, Disciple) land differently.

item.title: Roster & Parent Contacts
item.description:
Student roster and parent contact templates.

Two simple spreadsheets the lead teacher keeps current throughout the year.

Student roster

One row per confirmand:

  • Full name (including middle name — needed for Confirmation Sunday)
  • Preferred name (what they actually go by)
  • Grade / school
  • Baptism status — baptized already, or to be baptized at Confirmation
  • Baptism date (if known)
  • Mentor (assigned by late October)
  • Attendance — link to the attendance sheet
  • Statement of faith — draft / revised / final / read aloud
  • Photo collection — Y/N (for the Confirmation Sunday slideshow)

Parent contacts

One row per confirmand household:

  • Student name
  • Parent(s) / guardian(s) name
  • Phone(s)
  • Email(s)
  • Preferred contact channel — text, email, app
  • Household notes — divorced, shared custody, grandparent-in-the-loop, etc. (Keep this respectful and minimal.)

Privacy

This data is pastoral and should be treated as such. Don’t share it outside the Confirmation team. At the end of the year, archive in a secured location and clear the working copy.

📄
Downloadable templates for both sheets live alongside the calendar in the source repo. Your church may already have a preferred format — if so, use that.

item.title: Attendance
item.description:
A simple attendance tracker.

One row per student, one column per session. A few extra columns for retreat attendance, Serve Saturday, and the Confirmation Dessert.

Columns

  • Student name (preferred name for day-to-day, plus a separate full-name column for official records)
  • Each session date (1A, 1B, 2A, 2B, … 12)
  • Fall retreat
  • Serve Saturday
  • Confirmation Dessert

How to use it

  • Mark P (present), A (absent), or E (excused) each Sunday.
  • At the end of January and again in April, take five minutes with the roster — any student with more than 3 unexcused absences gets a phone call. Pastoral, not punitive.
  • The tracker is also useful when a parent asks in April: “How many classes has my kid missed?” and you can answer without guessing.
📄
Downloadable template lives with the other operational spreadsheets. Any simple tab-per-year spreadsheet works.

item.title: Group Chat Setup
item.description:
How we run the Confirmation group chat.

A group chat is the single most useful tool for keeping a Confirmation class connected between meetings. We use a purpose-built chat inside our church’s app so that every thread has an adult in it, parents can be looped in, and messages stay accessible to both students and leaders.

Instructions for Students

Hand this out at Kickoff (or screenshot and text to the class chat if it already exists):

  1. Download the church app. Search for your church name in the Apple App Store or Google Play Store.
  2. Press Get or Download.
  3. Open the app and click the person icon in the top-right corner.
  4. Press “Log in or sign up” and create an account.
  5. Once signed in, return to the home screen and press the messaging icon in the top-right corner.
  6. Press Discover and search Confirmation.
  7. Click the Confirmation group, click Join Group, and then View in Messaging.
  8. The group chat will now be available under Conversations.

If Your Church Doesn’t Have an App

Common alternatives:

  • GroupMe — platform-agnostic, doesn’t require a phone number to invite.
  • A private Discord server — easy for leaders to moderate channels; overkill for smaller classes.
  • Signal group — simple, secure, but requires a phone number.

Whichever you use: a leader is in the group, parents know it exists, and safe-practices guidelines are followed.

Ground Rules for the Chat

Write these up in the first message when the group is created:

  • A leader is always in this chat.
  • Talk like you would in front of an adult — because you are.
  • No bullying, no one-on-one DMs between adults and students.
  • Use the chat to share prayer requests, schedule reminders, and questions from class.

item.title: Teacher's Guide
item.description:
For the lead teacher.

The Sessions on this site are a Presbyterian-flavored adaptation of a published curriculum, Big God, Big Questions: Confirmation for a Growing Faith by Geneva Press (Presbyterian Church USA). That teacher’s guide, the BIG Journal (student workbook), the mentor guide, and the infographic poster set are the commercial resources we build on.

Where to buy

  • Curriculum overview
  • Teacher’s Guide
  • BIG Journal (student workbook)
  • Home / Mentor Guide
  • Infographics (set of 16 posters)
  • DVD / video set
  • Class Kit (everything bundled)

Free previews

  • Scope & Sequence PDF — the 12-session arc at a glance
  • Sample book — free to order
The published Big God, Big Questions curriculum is copyrighted by Geneva Press (© 2018, Louisville, KY). This site does not reproduce it. What’s published here — in the Sessions section — is our own teaching notes and adaptations for each topic, in our own words. If you plan to run a full Confirmation year, we strongly recommend buying the official resource: it’s thorough, theologically careful, and gives you the BIG Journal and infographics as student-facing pieces.

The arc at a glance

Twelve sessions, anchored in the four Big Questions asked at a PCUSA Confirmation:

  1. Trusting in the gracious mercy of God, do you turn from the ways of sin and renounce evil and its power in the world?
  2. Who is your Lord and Savior?
  3. Will you be Christ’s faithful disciple, obeying his Word and showing his love?
  4. Will you devote yourself to the church’s teaching and fellowship, to the breaking of bread and the prayers?

The twelve sessions map to those vows:

  1. Covenant — Joshua 24
  2. Trust — Psalm 105; Hebrews 11
  3. Sin — Matthew 22:34–40; Genesis 3
  4. Lord — Philippians 2; Exodus 3; 1 Kings 18
  5. Salvation — Ephesians 2; Exodus 3; Luke 19; 2 Kings 5; Mark 2; Matthew 18; Luke 15
  6. Disciple — Mark 8; Mark 12; Matthew 20; Acts 7
  7. Love — John 21
  8. Teaching — 2 Timothy 1; Hebrews 12
  9. Fellowship — 1 Corinthians 12; John 13
  10. Breaking Bread — Luke 22; Exodus 12; Acts 10
  11. Prayer — Luke 18; Colossians 4; 1 Thessalonians 5; Psalm 51; Romans 5
  12. Crossing Over — Joshua 3; Romans 6

Pacing

Most sessions are split into a Part A and Part B, taught across two class meetings two weeks apart. A handful (9, 10, 12) are single meetings. All told, expect ~22 class meetings plus a fall retreat, Confirmation Dessert, and Confirmation Sunday.

See the Sample Calendar for a concrete year’s worth of dates.

How to prep each session

Each session page on this site follows the actual flow a teacher works through:

  • Before class — supplies, setup, guests to invite
  • Opening / icebreaker — warm up the room
  • Read — the scripture + context you teach around
  • Discuss — the questions with answer hints for the teacher
  • Activity — the hands-on or group piece
  • Close — how to send them out

Read the page a few days ahead so you know what props to gather (clay, bread, bowls of water, a 2×4, the right video cued up). Many sessions have optional extensions you can pull in if discussion runs short.

Working with mentors

Every confirmand gets an adult mentor. Hand each mentor the Mentor’s Guide at the start of the year. The session pages flag where mentors are expected (Session 1, Session 4, Session 8, Session 12) — make sure those dates are on mentors’ calendars well in advance.

Working with parents

The rhythm of parent emails runs parallel to the sessions. Send the matching email the week after each topic. It gives parents a Home Practice to try with their confirmand and keeps the dinner-table theology going between classes.

The statement-of-faith project

Introduced in Session 12 and read aloud at the Confirmation Dessert. See the Statement of Faith section for the framework, guides, and examples.

item.title: Mentor's Guide
item.description:
For each mentor.

Every confirmand gets an adult mentor for the year — someone from the congregation who commits to meeting with, praying for, and eventually speaking a blessing over them at the Confirmation Dessert. Mentors are the single most underrated part of a Confirmation year.

We use the published Big God, Big Questions Home / Mentor Guide by Geneva Press. That resource is copyrighted and not reproduced on this site — give each mentor a copy. What follows is our own church-specific guidance on picking and supporting mentors.

Selecting mentors

From the Big God, Big Questions Teacher’s Guide, adapted:

  • A mentor needs to be accessible to their confirmand on a regular basis.
  • Except for unique circumstances, connect confirmands to mentors who are not members of their own family.
  • Take a young person’s pastoral needs into account when pairing.
  • The most important qualifications for a mentor are not piety or orthodoxy, but love and grace.
  • Every adult who works with children and youth must be screened through a criminal background check. Contact your congregation’s insurance representative.
  • Check with your pastor or Christian educator about your church’s safe-practices policy for youth. Meeting in a coffee shop or at church when others are present is usually the right default.

What mentors commit to

  • Attending the sessions where mentors are expected: Session 1 (Kickoff / Covenant), Session 4 (Lord), Session 8 (Teaching), and Session 12 (Crossing Over). The session pages flag these.
  • A monthly check-in with their confirmand — a coffee, a walk, or a shared activity. No agenda required beyond “how’s your faith going?”
  • Praying for their confirmand by name, every day, all year.
  • Speaking a blessing over their confirmand at the Confirmation Dessert.

Monthly rhythm

A simple pattern to share with mentors:

  • Early fall — introduce yourself. Find out what they like to do. Let them know what you’re praying for them about.
  • Mid-year — walk through the Big God, Big Questions mentor-guide prompt for the current session. Listen more than you talk.
  • Lent / spring — check in on the statement of faith. Be a safe person to read the first, terrified draft.
  • May — write your blessing for the Dessert. Show up. Mean it.

Supporting mentors

Mentor burnout is real. The lead teacher should:

  • Text mentors the week of each Confirmation class they’re expected at (don’t assume the calendar is enough).
  • Email mentors a 2-sentence summary after each session so they can bring it up naturally in their next check-in.
  • Check in with mentors by phone twice a year. Some need encouragement; some need to gracefully tag out.
